# /classroom-newsletter-draft

Produces a classroom newsletter for families that leads with the most useful information first and gives at least one concrete, low-effort action families can take at home.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this on whatever cadence the teacher sends family updates (weekly, biweekly, monthly) when there's real content to report - what the class is learning, upcoming dates, and anything families should know or do - rather than sending a content-free "just checking in" update.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- What's been covered in class recently and what's coming up in the next period (specific topics/units, not just subject names)
- Upcoming dates that need family action or awareness: tests, field trips, project due dates, half days, conference sign-ups
- Any specific ask for families this period (a permission slip, a home reading log, materials needed for a project)
- Grade level and family audience - affects tone (elementary newsletters often skew warmer/simpler; middle/high school updates can be more logistics-focused since students carry more responsibility themselves)

## Workflow

1. Lead with upcoming dates/action items before the "what we learned" recap - families skim newsletters for what they need to do or know, and burying that under a class recap means it gets missed.
2. Make the "what we're learning" section specific and concrete (name the actual topic, skill, or book) rather than a vague subject-level summary - specificity is what makes a newsletter feel worth reading regularly.
3. Include exactly one clear, low-effort "at home" suggestion tied to what's currently being taught (a conversation starter, a 5-minute activity) rather than a long list of suggestions families won't act on.
4. Flag any needed action items distinctly and visually (bolded, bulleted, separated) so a busy parent skimming on their phone can't miss a permission slip deadline or supply request.
5. Keep a consistent structure issue to issue so families learn where to look for what - predictability makes a recurring newsletter more useful, not less interesting.
6. End with an open invitation for questions/contact, reinforcing that the newsletter supplements rather than replaces direct communication about individual student concerns.

## Output format

- **Header**: class/grade, date range covered
- **Action items & upcoming dates** (bulleted, bolded where a deadline exists)
- **What we're learning**: specific topics/skills per subject, brief
- **At home this week**: one concrete, low-effort suggestion
- **Questions?**: contact invitation line

## Do
- Put action items and dates before the general recap content
- Name specific topics/skills rather than vague subject summaries
- Keep the "at home" section to one concrete, doable suggestion

## Don't
- Don't bury a permission slip or deadline inside a paragraph of recap content
- Don't send a content-free newsletter just to maintain cadence - skip or delay if there's nothing real to report
- Don't use this newsletter to raise an individual student's specific concern; that belongs in a direct parent email

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Action items and dates appear before the general class recap
- [ ] "What we're learning" names specific topics, not vague subject-level summaries
- [ ] Tone and complexity match the stated grade level and family audience
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /classroom-newsletter-draft or describe the task from the skill description.

metadata:

# /differentiated-assignment-variant

Produces one or more differentiated versions of an existing assignment - scaffolded, extended, or language-accessible - each still assessing the same core objective as the original.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when an existing assignment needs to flex for a range of learners in the same class - a student who needs more scaffolding to access the same content, an advanced student who needs a genuine extension (not just "more of the same"), or an English language learner who needs the same thinking task with reduced language barriers.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The original assignment in full (task, expected output, current reading/complexity level)
- Which variant(s) are needed: scaffolded/simplified, extension/enrichment, language-accessibility, or a combination
- The specific support need if known (a reading level, a specific IEP/504 accommodation type, an ELL proficiency level) - without needing or including any private student diagnosis details in the output itself
- The core objective that must stay identical across all versions, so the teacher can confirm the variants are still assessing the same thing

## Safety

- Do not include or infer specific student diagnoses, disability categories, or confidential details in the output - describe supports generically (e.g., "reduced text density," "sentence starters provided," "extended time built in") rather than referencing a specific diagnosis.
- Note explicitly that the teacher should apply their own judgment about which variant fits a specific student, and should cross-check any accommodation against that student's actual IEP/504 plan or their school's ELL support guidance, since this skill cannot see those documents.

## Workflow

1. Restate the core objective first and hold it constant - every variant must still measure the same underlying skill or understanding as the original, just through a different level of complexity or support; if a variant changes what's actually being assessed, it's not differentiation, it's a different assignment.
2. For a scaffolded/simplified variant: reduce reading/text density, chunk multi-step instructions into smaller explicit steps, add sentence starters or a word bank if writing is involved, and/or reduce the number of items/problems while keeping the same skill demand - don't just make it "easier" by lowering rigor on the actual thinking required.
3. For an extension/enrichment variant: add genuine complexity or open-endedness (a more ambiguous prompt, a requirement to justify/defend reasoning, an additional layer connecting to a related concept) - never just "the same thing, but more of it" (e.g., not just double the problems), since volume isn't rigor.
4. For a language-accessibility variant: simplify sentence structure and vocabulary in instructions (not the academic content/rigor itself), add visual supports or a glossary for key terms, and consider allowing a first-language response option for open-ended parts if appropriate - the cognitive task should stay as rigorous as the original.
5. Keep formatting and length parallel enough across variants that no version visibly stands out as "the easy one" or "the smart one" in a way students would notice if versions are distributed side by side in class.
6. Flag clearly for the teacher which specific elements changed in each variant versus the original, so it's easy to verify the core objective truly held constant.

## Output format

For each requested variant:
- **Variant name** (Scaffolded / Extension / Language-Accessible)
- **What changed vs. original**: specific bullet list (e.g., "reduced from 10 to 6 problems, same skill per problem"; "added a synthesis question requiring comparison across two texts")
- **Full assignment text** for that variant
- **Core objective check**: one line confirming what's still being assessed, unchanged

## Do
- Keep the core objective identical across every variant produced
- Make scaffolded versions easier through support and chunking, not through lowering the actual skill being assessed
- Make extension versions genuinely more complex, not just longer

## Don't
- Don't reference or infer a specific disability diagnosis in the output - describe supports generically
- Don't create a scaffolded version that quietly changes what's being assessed (that's a different assignment, not a differentiated one)
- Don't make different variants look visibly distinct in a way that singles out students in a shared classroom setting

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every variant is confirmed to assess the same core objective as the original
- [ ] No specific student diagnosis or confidential detail is referenced, only generic support types
- [ ] A note reminds the teacher to cross-check any accommodation against the actual student's IEP/504/ELL plan and apply their own judgment
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /differentiated-assignment-variant or describe the task from the skill description.

name: end-of-term-narrative-report-comments
description: >
  Writes full narrative report card comments (not just a comment bank of fragments) for an individual student, synthesizing the term's academic performance, growth, and specific next steps into a cohesive paragraph parents will actually read start to finish. Triggers: "write the full narrative comment for Emma's report card", "draft end-of-term comments for my whole class", /end-of-term-narrative-report-comments.
metadata:

  type: base

---
# /end-of-term-narrative-report-comments

Produces a complete, cohesive narrative report card comment for one student - synthesized into flowing paragraph(s), not a fragment bank - covering the term's performance, growth, and a specific forward-looking note.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this at the actual report-card-writing stage for a specific student, once the teacher has real information to synthesize (grades, notable moments, growth pattern) - this is the finished, ready-to-print comment, distinct from student-feedback-comment-bank which builds reusable starter fragments for many students at once during grading.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The student's actual performance this term: grades/levels by subject or skill area, and specific moments or patterns worth naming (a project that stood out, a consistent strength, a recurring struggle)
- The trajectory across the term, if known - did they start strong and taper, struggle early and improve, stay consistent - since a snapshot-only comment misses what parents most want to know (are things getting better or worse)
- Report card format constraints: character/word limit, whether it's one holistic comment or broken out by subject
- Anything sensitive that needs careful framing (a term marked by a family disruption, a diagnosed learning difference already known and on file, a significant behavior period) - handle generically and constructively, without overstating or diagnosing

## Safety

- Keep comments factual and specific to this student's actual documented performance - do not fabricate specific achievements, incidents, or grades not provided.
- Avoid any language that reads as a diagnosis or clinical judgment; if a learning difference is already documented and known, refer to it only in terms of accommodations/support already in place, not as new commentary on the condition itself.
- Note that the teacher should apply their own judgment about tone and specificity for this individual student and family - some families need more direct language about a struggle, others need more scaffolding around the same information; this draft is a starting point to personalize, not a final send-as-is document.

## Workflow

1. Open with a genuine, specific strength - not a generic "a pleasure to have in class" line unless it's actually true and the teacher wants it - since parents can tell a templated opener from a real one within the first sentence.
2. State the term's overall trajectory plainly (consistent, improving, declining, uneven) since this single piece of information is often what parents are most trying to extract from a report card comment.
3. Name one or two specific, concrete examples that illustrate the pattern described - a project, an assessment, a classroom moment - rather than staying at the level of abstract trait description the whole way through.
4. If there's a struggle or growth area to name, frame it constructively and specifically, paired with what's already being done about it or what the next concrete step is - a struggle named with no next step reads as a dead end rather than useful information.
5. Keep the paragraph flowing as connected prose, not a list of disconnected trait statements strung together with commas - this is a narrative, and it should read like one coherent assessment of the student's term.
6. Respect the stated length/format constraint exactly - trim content rather than exceeding a hard character limit, prioritizing the trajectory statement and one concrete example over exhaustive detail.

## Output format

- **Comment** (single flowing paragraph or the number of paragraphs matching the report format): specific opening strength → overall trajectory → 1-2 concrete examples → growth area with a next step (if applicable) → forward-looking close
- **Word/character count** noted against the stated limit
- **Personalization reminder**: a line noting the teacher should review and adjust tone/specificity for this exact student and family before finalizing

## Do
- Open with a genuine, specific strength rather than a stock opening line
- State the term's trajectory plainly (improving/declining/consistent), not just a snapshot
- Pair any named growth area with a concrete next step

## Don't
- Don't fabricate specific achievements, incidents, or numbers not provided by the teacher
- Don't use diagnostic or clinical language, even when a learning difference is already known and documented
- Don't exceed a stated character/word limit - trim rather than overflow

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Comment reads as connected narrative prose, not a list of disconnected traits
- [ ] Overall trajectory (improving/declining/consistent) is stated plainly, not just a snapshot
- [ ] No fabricated specifics, and no diagnostic/clinical language is used
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /end-of-term-narrative-report-comments or describe the task from the skill description.

## Works with

## When to use

Use this when a teacher already has a student's IEP/504 accommodations on file and needs to translate general accommodation language (e.g., "extended time," "reduced distraction environment," "chunked instructions") into specific steps for one concrete upcoming lesson or assignment, rather than re-reading the full legal document every time they plan.

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The specific accommodations already listed in the student's current, active IEP/504 (the teacher must supply these directly from the actual document - never guess, infer, or fill in typical accommodations that aren't confirmed as this student's actual plan)
- The specific lesson or assignment this needs to apply to
- Whether this is for a general classroom lesson, a test/quiz, or a specific assignment type, since accommodations translate differently to each context
- Confirmation that the source accommodations are current (IEPs/504s are reviewed periodically and can change)

## Safety

- This skill only works from accommodations the teacher explicitly provides from the student's actual, current IEP/504 - it must never invent, assume, or generalize accommodations for a student based on a diagnosis category or typical case; if the teacher hasn't supplied the specific documented accommodations, stop and ask for them rather than guessing.
- This is a working translation tool, not a legal or compliance document - the actual IEP/504 remains the binding document, and any conflict between this note and the official plan defers to the official plan.

- **This lesson/assignment**: name and date
- **Translated action items**: table - Accommodation | Specific action for this lesson | Advance prep needed (if any)

- Don't include diagnostic or clinical language in the output

- [ ] Every accommodation used was explicitly provided by the teacher, none invented or assumed

# /lesson-plan-outline

Produces a single-period lesson plan outline sequenced by actual instructional time, not just a topic summary - with a clear objective, hook, teaching sequence, practice, and a real check for understanding.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when planning an individual lesson (not a full unit) - prepping for tomorrow, filling in a gap in an existing unit, or building a demo/observation lesson that needs to show clear structure.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Subject, grade level, and the specific topic/standard for this one lesson (not the whole unit)
- Class period length (affects how much can realistically fit - a 40-minute period and a 90-minute block need different pacing)
- Where this lesson sits in the sequence: is it an introduction to the topic, reinforcement, or a review/synthesis lesson - this changes what the hook and practice should emphasize
- Any known class-specific factor that affects pacing (large class, students with an IEP/504 needing built-in flexibility, a lesson right before a break)

## Workflow

1. Write the objective as a single, observable statement of what students will be able to do by the end (not "students will learn about..." - use a concrete, checkable action verb).
2. Build a hook that takes 3-5 minutes max and connects directly to the objective - a question, a quick demo, a real-world tie-in - not a generic icebreaker unrelated to the day's content.
3. Sequence direct instruction in small chunks with a built-in check after each chunk, rather than one long uninterrupted explanation followed by a single check at the end - this catches confusion while it's still fixable in the same period.
4. Include guided practice before independent practice, explicitly named as separate steps, so the plan shows the gradual release of responsibility rather than jumping straight to solo work.
5. Build in one genuine check-for-understanding moment with a real mechanism (quick poll, exit ticket, cold-call with a specific question) - not just "monitor students," which isn't actionable or repeatable by a substitute.
6. Time-stamp every section against the actual period length provided, and make sure the total adds up realistically with a few minutes of buffer, not an idealized 100%-efficient period.
7. Close with a specific closure activity tied back to the objective (not just "review" - name what gets reviewed and how) and note what the exit ticket or check-out mechanism is.

## Output format

- **Objective**: one observable, checkable statement
- **Standard/goal alignment**: the specific standard or learning goal this maps to
- **Timing table**: Section | Minutes | What happens (Hook, Instruction chunk 1, Check, Instruction chunk 2, Guided practice, Independent practice, Closure/exit ticket)
- **Materials needed**: concrete list
- **Differentiation note**: one line on how this lesson flexes for the class-specific factor mentioned, if any
- **Teacher judgment note**: a line reminding the teacher to adjust pacing/depth based on their actual students' current level, which this plan can't fully know

## Do
- Time-stamp every section against the real period length given
- Build in a check for understanding after each instructional chunk, not just at the end
- Write the objective as something observable and checkable, not a vague topic description

## Don't
- Don't front-load a long uninterrupted lecture block with no mid-lesson checks
- Don't skip guided practice and jump straight from instruction to independent work
- Don't assume a specific class's prior knowledge level - flag it as something the teacher should judge

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Timing adds up realistically to the stated period length
- [ ] At least one concrete, actionable check-for-understanding mechanism is included
- [ ] A note reminds the teacher to apply their own judgment about their specific students' level and needs
```

  Drafts a parent/guardian email about a specific student situation (academic concern, behavior incident, positive recognition, or general update), calibrated in tone and detail to the seriousness of the situation and written to invite partnership rather than assign blame. Triggers: "write an email to a parent about their kid falling behind in math", "draft a note home about today's incident", /parent-communication-email.

# /parent-communication-email

Produces a parent/guardian email about a specific student situation, written to invite the parent as a partner in the solution rather than deliver a verdict - calibrated to the actual seriousness of the situation.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this any time a specific student's situation warrants a home email - a grade slipping, a behavior incident, a positive moment worth recognizing, or a general update - when the message needs to land well with a parent who wasn't in the room and may already be anxious or defensive.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Student's name/pronoun and the specific situation, described factually (what happened, when, any pattern vs. one-off)
- The purpose of this email: informing, requesting a meeting/call, asking the parent to reinforce something at home, or purely positive recognition
- Severity/urgency level - this changes format (a quick heads-up vs. a documented concern that may need to be more formal and specific)
- Anything already known about this student/family relationship that affects tone (first contact vs. ongoing dialogue, any known sensitivities)

## Safety

- Note that this is a draft for the teacher's own judgment and district communication norms - the teacher should adjust identifying details, tone, and formality to match their school's policies and their knowledge of the specific student and family.
- For anything involving a possible safety concern, suspected abuse/neglect indicator, or serious behavioral incident, this email should not be the first or only step - follow the school's mandatory reporting and administrative notification procedures first; this skill only drafts the parent-facing communication piece.
- Keep language about the student factual and behavior-specific ("Sam left his seat during the quiz three times") rather than character-judging ("Sam was disruptive and disrespectful") - this reduces defensiveness and keeps the email focused on observable events.

## Workflow

1. Open with a clear, calm statement of purpose in the first sentence or two - parents skim for the headline first; don't bury why you're writing under pleasantries.
2. Describe the situation using specific, observable facts (what happened, when, how often) rather than character judgments or vague generalizations - this is both fairer to the student and less likely to trigger a defensive reply.
3. For a concern-based email, include what's already been tried at school before this email was sent, so the parent knows this isn't the first line of response and understands the current context.
4. State clearly and specifically what you're asking the parent to do, if anything (reinforce a routine at home, have a conversation, attend a call) - an email with no clear ask often gets read and shelved without follow-through.
5. For a positive-recognition email, be just as specific as a concern email would be - name the actual moment or behavior, not a generic "great student" comment, since specificity is what makes praise land as genuine.
6. Close by inviting a two-way conversation (offering a call, welcoming their perspective) rather than ending on a unilateral statement, which reinforces that the teacher sees the parent as a partner, not just an audience.
7. Keep length appropriate to severity: a quick positive note can be 3-4 sentences; a documented concern may need more detail but should still stay focused and avoid over-explaining.

## Output format

- **Subject line**: specific and calm, not alarming (avoid "URGENT" unless genuinely urgent)
- **Opening**: purpose stated plainly
- **Situation**: factual, observable description
- **Context** (if concern-based): what's already been tried
- **The ask**: specific and clear, or none if purely informational
- **Closing**: invitation for two-way dialogue

## Do
- Describe behavior/events factually and specifically rather than in character-judging language
- State a clear, specific ask when one exists
- Make positive-recognition emails just as specific as concern-based ones

## Don't
- Don't use this as a substitute for required school reporting procedures on safety/welfare concerns
- Don't send a concern email with no context on what's already been tried at school
- Don't end on a one-way statement - always leave the door open for the parent's perspective

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Situation is described in factual, observable language, not character judgments
- [ ] A clear ask is stated if one exists, or explicitly noted as informational-only if not
- [ ] Note included that the teacher should apply judgment about tone/formality for this specific student and family, and that safety concerns follow school reporting procedures first
```

## Works with

Use this when creating grading criteria for a specific assignment (essay, project, presentation, lab report) before it's assigned - ideally early enough that the rubric can be shared with students up front, not just used for grading after the fact.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The assignment itself in enough detail to know what's actually being assessed (a persuasive essay assesses different things than a lab report or a group presentation)
- Grade level, since descriptor language and expectations need to match what's realistic for that age/skill level
- The specific criteria that matter most for this assignment (e.g., for a persuasive essay: claim clarity, evidence use, counterargument handling, organization, conventions) rather than a generic one-size-fits-all set
- Point scale or performance-level count needed (4-point, 3-point, checkbox-style) and total point value if it needs to map to a grade

## Workflow

1. Identify 3-5 criteria that are actually specific to this assignment's learning goals - resist defaulting to a generic "content/organization/grammar" template that could apply to any writing assignment; name the criteria this specific task is actually testing.
2. For each criterion, write distinct, observable descriptors at each performance level - describe what the work actually looks like or contains at that level (e.g., "uses two or more pieces of evidence, each explicitly linked back to the claim" vs. just "good use of evidence").
3. Make sure each performance level is genuinely distinguishable from its neighbors - if the difference between "proficient" and "developing" is just the adjective, rewrite until there's a concrete, checkable difference a student could self-assess against.

6. Add the total point value or grade-mapping if requested, and double check the math adds up cleanly to the target total.

- **Total possible points**
- **Teacher judgment note**: a line noting the teacher should adjust language/expectations to match their specific students' current skill level and any accommodations in place

# /student-feedback-comment-bank

Produces a bank of specific, actionable feedback comment starters organized by common performance patterns, meant to be personalized per student rather than pasted verbatim for everyone.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this ahead of grading a stack of assignments or writing report card comments, when the teacher needs a starting structure for common patterns (strong performance, inconsistent effort, specific skill gap, notable growth) that still leaves room to personalize each one - not a set of comments meant to be copied unchanged.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The assignment or reporting period this is for (specific essay, unit test, or general report-card period) and the subject/grade level
- The specific skills or criteria being commented on (matches a rubric if one exists) so comments are concrete rather than generic
- The tone/audience: comments for the student to read directly, for a parent audience, or both
- Any specific performance patterns already observed across the class that the teacher wants comment starters for (e.g., "half my class nailed the thesis but struggled with evidence")

## Safety

- Comments are starting templates only - the teacher must personalize each one with the specific student's actual work before using it; a comment bank used verbatim and unedited reads as impersonal and can undermine trust with students and parents.
- Keep language age-appropriate for the stated grade level, and note that the teacher should adjust tone/directness based on their knowledge of the individual student (some students respond better to very direct feedback, others need more encouragement framing for the same underlying point).
- Avoid comparative language between students (no "unlike some students...") - every comment should stand on that one student's own work.

## Workflow

1. Organize comment starters by performance pattern (e.g., strong/proficient, developing with a specific gap, inconsistent, notable growth, needs significant support) rather than by letter grade, since the same grade can reflect different underlying patterns needing different feedback.
2. Write each comment starter with a clear bracketed placeholder for the specific, personalizing detail (e.g., "Your analysis of [specific text/example] showed strong..." ) so the teacher is prompted to insert something real rather than leaving it generic.
3. Pair every area-for-growth comment with a specific, actionable next step the student can act on (not just "needs to improve organization" - give the starter a slot for naming what specifically to try next).
4. Keep language strengths-first even for lower-performing patterns - lead with what is working before naming the gap, since this format is both more accurate (there's usually something working) and better received by students and parents.
5. Match the reading/reception level to the stated audience - student-facing comments should use language the student themselves can understand and act on; parent-facing comments can carry slightly more context.
6. Include one flexible "growth over time" comment starter even if not explicitly requested, since report cards especially benefit from acknowledging trajectory, not just a snapshot.

## Output format

Organized by performance pattern, each with 2-3 comment starters:
- **Strong/Proficient performance**
- **Developing - specific skill gap** (sub-grouped by common skill gaps relevant to this assignment/subject)
- **Inconsistent performance**
- **Notable growth over time**
- **Needs significant support**

Each starter includes a `[bracketed placeholder]` for the personalizing detail, and a reminder line: "Personalize the bracketed detail with this student's actual work before sending - do not use unedited."

## Do
- Include a bracketed placeholder in every comment prompting a real, specific detail
- Lead every comment with a strength before naming a growth area
- Pair growth-area comments with a specific, actionable next step

## Don't
- Don't write comments generic enough to apply to any student without editing - that defeats the purpose
- Don't use comparative language between students
- Don't let this be used verbatim without personalization - flag that requirement clearly

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every comment starter includes a bracketed placeholder for a personalizing detail
- [ ] Growth-area comments include a specific, actionable next step, not just a named deficiency
- [ ] A reminder is included that comments must be personalized per student before use, and language matches the stated grade level
```

# /substitute-teacher-day-plan

Produces a day plan written for someone who has never met these students and doesn't know the classroom's unwritten routines - explicit enough to run cold, with contingencies built in.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this for a planned absence (with lead time to prep something specific) or an emergency/sick-day plan built from a generic template the teacher keeps on hand - either way, the substitute has no context the regular teacher takes for granted.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Class periods/schedule for the day and what's actually being covered in each (or, if this is a generic emergency plan, note that it should be low-prep/self-contained content usable on any day)
- Known classroom routines a substitute wouldn't otherwise know: attendance procedure, where materials are kept, bathroom/hall pass system, what to do with cell phones, dismissal procedure
- Any students who need a specific heads-up (a 504/IEP accommodation the sub must honor, a student who serves as a reliable helper, any known behavior pattern the sub should be aware of) - describe generically and behaviorally, not by diagnosis
- Whether this is a planned absence (content can be tightly tied to where the class actually is) or an emergency plan (needs to be self-contained, low-prep, and not dependent on knowing exactly where the unit left off)

## Workflow

1. Open with a quick-reference block at the very top: bell schedule, attendance procedure, and the single most important "if X happens, do Y" line (fire drill procedure location, who to call for help) - a substitute needs this before anything else, not buried on page three.
2. Write instructions as literal scripts where it matters - exact words to say when introducing an activity, not just "explain the assignment," since the substitute doesn't know the class's usual framing or vocabulary.
3. Time-stamp every activity generously (pad the substitute's unfamiliarity with routines into the timing, not just the regular teacher's usual pace) and always include a "if you finish early" backup activity for every block.
4. Make every activity as self-directed as possible - worksheets, videos with specific stop-and-discuss points, independent reading - rather than anything requiring subject-matter judgment calls the substitute isn't equipped to make.
5. Name specific student helpers generically ("ask a student to help pass out papers" is fine; naming a specific reliable student by name is even more useful if known) and flag any known accommodation needs behaviorally (e.g., "this student may need extra time - allow it without discussion") without naming a diagnosis.
6. Include an explicit end-of-day checklist for the substitute (collect materials, leave a note for the teacher, submit attendance) so nothing gets lost in the handoff back.
7. Leave a dedicated space for the substitute to leave feedback/notes for the returning teacher - how the day actually went, not just whether the plan was followed.

## Output format

- **Quick reference** (top of doc): bell schedule, attendance procedure, emergency procedure/contact
- **Period-by-period plan**: Time | Activity | Script/instructions to read aloud | Materials needed | If-finished-early backup
- **Classroom routines**: passes, phones, dismissal
- **Student-specific notes**: generic, behavioral, no diagnoses
- **End-of-day checklist** for the substitute
- **Notes back to the teacher**: blank space for the sub to fill in

## Do
- Write literal scripts for anything requiring specific framing or vocabulary
- Pad timing estimates since a substitute doesn't know the class's usual pace
- Include an early-finisher backup for every single activity block

## Don't
- Don't assume the substitute knows any classroom routine, however obvious it seems to the regular teacher
- Don't reference a student's diagnosis or confidential file details - describe needs behaviorally and generically
- Don't build a plan reliant on judgment calls only the regular teacher could make

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every activity block includes an early-finisher backup
- [ ] Emergency/quick-reference info is placed at the very top, not buried
- [ ] No student diagnosis or confidential detail is referenced; needs are described generically and behaviorally
```
